author: [SLY] Groove [STAR]
content:
As an example, I'm not developing tools for Holosim because I don't enjoy the gameplay. I have a prototype which alerts on approaching enemy fleets. I have one which runs away if certain power thresholds are observed. I considered an attack optimization tool for focused and synchronized fire. But then I wondered - what's left for me to do? If I can automate enough of the game so that my bot can play the game competively, what's the point?

My hope is that there will be enough "fun things" to do that it'll make sense to automate the boring/grindy aspects and give me more time to do the fun/engaging/entertaining things.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I think the main issues here would be emissions and limited slot availability. Over time, every T1 would eventually become a T5, which would erode the asset’s “premium” nature. In that scenario, a T4 might actually be preferable, since all T5 slots would already be occupied.

To make CS upgradeable, you would need a burn mechanism to balance the upgrades, which we know for sure won’t be there until HRZ in few years.

In any case, it would be a significant amount of work for Chris (and a lot of complaints either way).

author: ROME | Ghaz OutroONI
content: Interesting. It's always good to know what happened, so we can learn from it. I did an extensive research and the summary is: > In essence, Influence tried to launch a full sandbox economy from day one with a tiny player base, no NPC scaffolding, heavy blockchain friction, and a token that could be immediately sold for real money — on a Layer 2 chain that was itself bleeding users. Each problem amplified the others. The root cause was trying to build an EVE Online–scale economy without any of EVE's 20 years of guardrails, player density, or accessible onboarding. Then I fed the whole research into another research "What can Star Atlas learn from it?". Ok, this gave me a very, very long explanation. Or in other words: The good, the bad and the ugly. I don't know if I should post it here, don't wanna FUD my own bags  But I'm pretty sure that C4 will solve most (if not all) of the problems. Can't wait.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
I put about $2000 into it.  Their economy relied on new players coming in.  New players did not come in.  Current players left as the economy became stagnant and started dying. They didn't attempt to change anything. 

Never made it to combat so all bases were essentially immortal. (Didn't help the economy.)

They also didn't have a revenue model in place to make money from current players.  And they started laying off all their staff within 3 months.  Just a single guy left.

Refused to change anything in the game and blamed their poor player retention on their on ramp providers.  Took almost a year and they kept getting rugged from on ramp providers.

What else.... They were on Starknet which is an eth L2.  They started running into problems when Eth got too expensive.  It became too expensive to play.

They had a foundation which was only funded by their token.  The foundation wasn't setup but had a ton of $$$ sitting on it.  By the time it was setup the $$$ was all gone.

Sad story.  The orbital mechanics were top tier.  Maybe too realistic.  Flying to an asteroid could take 7 irl days.  Crafting loops some of the most complicated I've seen.

I actually think the game is still live and the plan is to open source it to the community.

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content: As a non-coder, I've found the process to be highly iterative. Last mile is the biggest challenge, which includes QA and polish, and validating that everything works as intended. Generally, the first prompt is going to deliver a shell of an app with a ton of mock data that initially looks good on the surface. Then you start testing out features and conclude that most of the shit isn't actually wired up, especially with onchain mechanics. And so you go through multiple rounds of scoping and outlining what isn't working, how it's supposed to work, deploying, and testing again. I'm doing all of this in Discord via OpenClaw. But I'll emphasize again, the final step of full QA testing is what is taking me the longest on everything. But that's also because I want production grade consumer ready applications/features. If I'm building personal tools, I wouldn't put it through nearly the same degree of scrutiny.  This is what this article is pointing out, I think. You can take apps 80-90% of the way pretty easily. The last 10-20% require at least a basic understanding of tech development, and an eye for detail to sift through the broken pieces. From my perspective, this is already how I worked. 60% of my time was spent on meetings with devs, discussing product goals and feature requirements. Then we would review the work they would do. I'd give feedback, and we'd continue that process until an app was delivered. The biggest differences for me are two-fold: 1) The time between iterations is compressed from months (years?) down to days or weeks, and 2) the pace at which something gets built is bottlenecked entirely by me. I'm the one responsible for driving it forward, and the more time I put into it, the faster it gets built.   I've stated this previously, but in over 10 years of running cryto tech companies, I've never had that level of empowerment (maybe I should have learned to code). It was largely me waiting on my team to build the thing. I feel fully unlocked now, at the cost of some of the more public-facing aspects of the role I performed before. But right now, executing and delivering is our top priority.

All that said, this was much more fluid with Anthropic. Now that they've cut off all ingress to OpenClaw users, at least with a subscription plan, I migrated over to Codex/GPT. author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: imo Hermes has a far better concept than OpenClaw. But Hermes was a security nightmare ... agents started inside a cron job were able to use the full set of commands, including all shell commands. So if you e.g. let Hermes read your mails, someone could do a prompt injection and delete all your files. lol While things have improved in the meantime, I would only run it fully isolated in a Docker container. Also it had numerous flaws, like it injected the progress markers into the SSE content of UI clients like Open WebUI. So after some time the LLM thought it can just call tools by writing "Cron: List". And in the UI it looked like the normal tool call, but it was just text. And then it hallucinated the result from the tool. That sneaky bastard  It took me days to find this out (see my issue 6972).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Most vibe coders underestimate how important it is not to just start coding right away. You say something to Claude and no matter how bad your plan is, Claude will just start it (in most cases). Pro tip from me  ... the 2 most important things I do: Start with "Don't change anything yet, we are now discussing/brainstorming the following topic and we try to find flaws and improvements: ...",Then it goes back and forth until we have a (seemingly) foolproof plan,When the plan is finished, I still don't start it. I instead use the following prompt to let Claude double check everything (a sub agent then challenges the plan and tries to find problems). This works surprisingly well (even if you use the same LLM):,Create a briefing document of our problem and plan. Include: - The problem and our intended approach - Constraints and requirements - Alternatives we considered and why we rejected them - Open questions or uncertainties  Pass that briefing to a sub-agent for critical review. The sub-agent must respond in this exact format: RISKS: [things that could go wrong or break] GAPS: [anything we missed, forgot, or didn't consider] VERDICT: PROCEED or REVISE DETAILS: [explanation and suggestions]  If the verdict is REVISE, review the sub-agent's concerns, tell me which ones you agree with, and propose an updated plan.

author: [COEX] 𝙈𝙪𝙖𝙙'𝙙𝙞𝙗
SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: @Eoganacht | BULK I regret to say that I do not agree with even a single sentence you wrote in the Foundation. In the parallel universe you inhabit, alt accounts might be common in RTS games, but in this universe, they are actively fought against.  Take Travian, for example a game with over 1.5 million players and incredibly strict anti-multi-account policies. The same applies to Gameforge titles, which boast over 400 million registered players worldwide. We played Ogame, one of their flagship titles, for years back in the 2000s, and it maintained a zero-tolerance policy toward multi-accounting. Beyond that, even 'account sitting' is banned in most of these games; it is only permitted under strict moderator supervision with special authorization. It is truly ridiculous that we are here today debating whether multi-accounting is legitimate.  It seems it will take you some time to realize that your 'parallel reality' does not apply here. However, the cost of this oversight will very likely be a stagnant player base. If there is one universal human trait, it is a shared dislike for rowing in vain. A game environment that supports multi-accounting creates exactly that feeling. Over time, players lose interest as they feel their efforts are futile. Please, when discussing these matters, try to think 5, 10, or even 50 steps ahead.

We're still at like 0.3% of the population using advanced coding tools via AI, which is one of the highest token usage activities. These guys are actually limited on compute. Prices are likely to increase. Longer term, I think we'll see better economies of scale and cost reduction across the board. But that could be 2-3 years, or longer for these data centers to come online.

content:
While Crane blames Claude and the Railway cloud system, I think the main lesson here is:
1) Backup often 2) Backup regularly 3) Backup as much as possible
Or even better: Use BTRFS with hourly snapshots and additionally do an external backup at least once a week (this is what I am doing).
If your latest backup is 3 months old, you are definitely doing something wrong, especially if you give an agent access to your production infrastructure.
Be careful out there!
